title: About
text: Hi I am Lucas! Welcome to my blog about intersting sports things, mostly biased to my favorite teams.

title: Nebraska had a program first in March Madness
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)

Nebraska’s March Madness run was one for the ages. The Husker’s secured their first March Madness win in program history over Troy. They came into the tournament being the only power 4 team to have never won a tournament game


Code
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = disp, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point()

title: From Champions of Europe to Constant Coaching Carousells: What Happened to Chelsea FC?
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)
library(ggbump)
library(ggrepel)
library(gt)
library(ggalt)
library(ggbeeswarm)
CLSeason <- read_csv("MajorProject/Pl2020_21.csv")
Season2022 <-read_csv("MajorProject/Pl2021_22.csv")
Season2023 <-read_csv("MajorProject/Pl2022_23.csv")
Season2024 <-read_csv("MajorProject/Pl2023_24.csv")
Season2025 <-read_csv("MajorProject/Pl2024_25.csv")
transfers <- read.csv("MajorProject/Pl_transfer_spending_2022_26.csv")
Ranks <- read.csv("MajorProject/PL_ranks.csv")


Chelsea FC had finally climbed the mountain again in 2021. With a 1-0 win over Manchester City on a faithful night at the Estádio do Dragão in Portugal, the Pride of London retook their throne as Champions of Europe. With a solid core, a great coach in Tomas Tuchel, and the full support of the ownership, they seemed to be untouchable. The thing that brought them down… the Russian Invasion of Ukraine.
You might be wondering how that could possibly bring an English football team from the top of the mountain to a staggering 12th place the next season. After the invasion started in 2022, the English FA forced Russian billionaire Ruben Abramovich, the owner of Chelsea to sell to Clearlake Captial and Todd Bohley, a group that would soon be known as Blueco.
Todd and Clearlake had an idea. This idea was a project to build a dynasty for years to come. They purchased about every player under the sun, however, they were all under age 25. Managers found this controversial due to lack of experience, however it was the decision of Blueco at the end of the day.
As we enter year 4 of the Chelsea project, are on their 6th manager, billions spent in transfer fees, and no league titles or champions leagues to show for it.
Lets dive into some of the numbers from their championship winning year versus recent years to identify where the glaring holes are to see if money can patch them starting with goal differential:


Code
CLDumbell <- ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=CLSeason, 
    aes(
      y=Squad, yend=Squad,
      x=GF, xend=GA),
    color="grey",
    linewidth = 1.5
  )  + 
  geom_point(
    data=CLSeason,
    aes(
      y=Squad,
      x=GF,
      color="Goals For"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=CLSeason,
    aes(
      y=Squad,
      x=GA,
      color="Goals Against"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  scale_color_manual(name="Goals", values=c("Goals Against" ="red", "Goals For"="green")) +
  labs(
    title = "Goals for and against in Chelsea's\nChampions League winning season",
    subtitle = "This is the easiest baseline to see what happened to the team.\nDid they stop scoring? Did they let in more goals? Lets find out:",
    y = "",
    x = "Goals For/ Goals Against CL Season",
    caption="Source: FBRef| By Lucas Krapfl"
  )+
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Dumbell23 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=Season2023, 
    aes(
      y=Squad, yend=Squad,
      x=GF, xend=GA),
    color="grey",
    linewidth = 1.5
  )  + 
  geom_point(
    data=Season2023,
    aes(
      y=Squad,
      x=GF,
      color="Goals For"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=Season2023,
    aes(
      y=Squad,
      x=GA,
      color="Goals Against"
    ),
    size=2
  ) + 
  scale_color_manual(name="Goals", values=c("Goals Against" ="red", "Goals For"="green")) +
  labs(
    title = "",
    subtitle = "",
    y = "",
    x = "Goals For/ Goals Against 22-23",
  )+
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )
CLDumbell + Dumbell23









Right off the bat we can see that the goal differential took a massive hit when key players started to leave, specifically their defensive anchor Antonio Rudiger. This poor performance would also lead to the departure of basically the entire offense in Kai Havertz, Mason Mount, Christian Pulisic, Mateo Kovacic, and more. Lets take another look at just how bad Chelsea’s squad got under Blueco.


Code
Chelsea23 <- Season2023 |> filter(Squad == "Chelsea")
ChelseaCL<- CLSeason |> filter(Squad == "Chelsea") 
Chelsea24<- Season2024 |> filter(Squad == "Chelsea") 
CLBubble<- ggplot() + 
  geom_point(
    data=CLSeason, 
    aes(x=GF, y=GA, size=GD), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) + 
  geom_point(
    data=ChelseaCL, 
    aes(x=GF, y=GA, size=GD), 
    color="blue") +
labs(
    title = "Chelsea goal differential\ndecline under Blueco",
    subtitle = "The bubble chart will show us if they moved along either axis or even\nswitched quadrants during Blueco's first year:",
    y = "Goals Against",
    x = "Goals For CL",
      size = "Goal Differential",
   caption="Source: FBRef | By Lucas Krapfl" ) +
   theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold")
    ) +
  geom_text_repel(data=ChelseaCL, aes(x=GF, y=GA, label=Squad)) +
  annotate("rect", fill = "blue", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 50, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 30, ymax = 40) +
  geom_text(aes(x=65, y=41, label="Great Teams"), size=3) 
  
Bubble2023 <- ggplot() + 
  labs(
    size = "Goal Differential",
    y = "Goals Against",
    x = "Goals For 22/23",
    caption="Source: FBRef | By Lucas Krapfl"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold")
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data=Season2023, 
    aes(x=GF, y=GA, size=GD), 
    color="grey", 
    alpha=.3) + 
  geom_point(
    data=Chelsea23, 
    aes(x=GF, y=GA, size=GD), 
    color="blue") +
  geom_text_repel(data=Chelsea23, aes(x=GF, y=GA, label=Squad)) +
  annotate("rect", fill = "blue", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 60, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 30, ymax = 40) +
  geom_text(aes(x=82, y=37, label="Great Teams"), size=3)
CLBubble + Bubble2023









This is shows that even after they spent a billion pounds on all the flashiest names to score all the cool highlight goals, you cannot win championships without a key defense. This project was proposed under the guise of development of young players, however when you fire the best developmental coach in the land and replace with 5 more yes men, clearly it does not work. Lets see if in the first season after Tuchel was fired if the coaching or the veteran leadership failed the project:


Code
CLTable <- CLSeason |>
  select(Squad, Rank, Avg_Age, Pts_per_MP) |>
  arrange((Rank))

Table2023 <- Season2023 |>
  select(Squad, Rank, Avg_Age, Pts_per_MP) |>
  arrange((Rank))

Table2024 <- Season2024 |>
  select(Squad, Rank, Avg_Age, Pts_per_MP) |>
  arrange((Rank))

CLTable |>
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    Rank = "Final Rank",
    Avg_Age = "Avg. Player Age",
    Pts_per_MP= "Points Per Match"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Did the decrease in veterans contribute to the downfall of Chelsea?\n(Title Season)",
    subtitle = "When most of the veteran leaders left the club, with young players joining, how did the rankings change:"
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("Source: FbRef | By Lucas Krapfl")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
     locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
     style = list(
       cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
       cell_text(weight = "bold", size=12)
     )
   ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "blue"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
      ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = Squad == "Chelsea")
  )

While clearly having veterans matters, as highlighted by the points per match, the most important change was not someone on the pitch. It doesn’t matter who your players are if you don’t have a good coach, PREFEREABLY NOT A YES-MAN FOR BLUECO. When you sack the best manager you have had it will not matter if you have veterans or not, you will lose. Lets take a look at what they did the year after their spending spree:


Code
CLBeeswarm <- ggplot() + 
  geom_jitter(
    data=CLSeason, 
    aes(x=Avg_Age, y=Pts), color="grey") + 
  geom_jitter(
    data=ChelseaCL, 
    aes(x=Avg_Age, y=Pts), color="Blue") + 
  geom_text_repel(
    data=ChelseaCL, 
    aes(x=Avg_Age, y=Pts, label=Squad)) +
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) +
  labs(
    title = "How many points Chelsea finished the\nseason with by average age",
    subtitle = "This will show us how many points each Chelsea Squad scored in leauge play relative\nto their team setup. The Champions Leauge squad was built around middle-age vets while the \nproject squad is $1 billon youngsters",
    y = "Points",
    x = "Average Age CL Season",
   caption="Source: FBRef | By Lucas Krapfl" )+
   theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold")
    ) +
  annotate("rect", fill = "cyan", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 23, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 80, ymax = Inf) +
  geom_text(aes(x=25, y=85, label="Man City Tier"), size=3) +
  annotate("rect", fill = "green", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 23, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 64.5, ymax = 80) +
  geom_text(aes(x=24, y=75, label="Title Contenders"), size=3) +
  annotate("rect", fill = "yellow", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 23, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 60, ymax = 64.5) +
  geom_text(aes(x=24, y=63, label="UCL Race"), size=3) + 
  annotate("rect", fill = "red", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 23, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 57, ymax = 60) +
  geom_text(aes(x=24, y=59, label="Just Missed UCL"), size=3) 

Beeswarm24 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_jitter(
    data=Season2024, 
    aes(x=Avg_Age, y=Pts), color="grey") + 
  geom_jitter(
    data=Chelsea24, 
    aes(x=Avg_Age, y=Pts), color="Blue") + 
  geom_text_repel(
    data=Chelsea24, 
    aes(x=Avg_Age, y=Pts, label=Squad)) +
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) +
labs(
    y = "Points",
    x = "Average Age Year 2 (23/24)" )+
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    ) +
   annotate("rect", fill = "green", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 23, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 73, ymax = Inf) +
  geom_text(aes(x=27, y=85, label="Title Contenders"), size=3) +
  annotate("rect", fill = "yellow", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 23, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 65, ymax = 73) +
  geom_text(aes(x=27, y=72, label="Champions Leauge Race"), size=3) +
 annotate("rect", fill = "red", alpha = 0.1, 
        xmin = 23, xmax = Inf,
        ymin = 57, ymax = 65) +
  geom_text(aes(x=26.5, y=63, label="Just Missed UCL"), size=3) 
CLBeeswarm + Beeswarm24









While this is a vast improvement from 12th place the year before, you are talking about Chelsea FC, a team that is used to winning the biggest and best trophies, competing in the Europa Conference League on WEEKDAYS. Oh and that thing I said about managers clashing with the sporting directors… after digging the team out of this hole the sporting directors fired him for asking for defenders at the end of the season!
However for how much they have spent… this is not good enough. Lets put into perspective just how much they have spent since Blueco took over:


Code
TransferSummary <- transfers |>
  group_by(Squad) |>
  summarise(
    Total_Spent = sum(Spent_M),
    Total_Players_In = sum(Players_In)
  )
ChelseaTransfers <- TransferSummary |> filter(Squad == "Chelsea FC")
ChelseaSpend <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=TransferSummary, aes(x=reorder(Squad, Total_Spent), weight=Total_Spent)) +
  geom_bar(data=ChelseaTransfers, aes(x=reorder(Squad, Total_Spent), weight=Total_Spent), fill="blue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Transfer market total spending since 2022",
    subtitle = "Since 2022 Chelsea have signed A LOT of players, but so has everyone else.\nWith all this spending you think there would be some big trophies to show for it...",
    y = "Transfer Spending 22-26 (Millions)",
    x = "Squad",
   caption="Source: TransferMarket | By Lucas Krapfl" )+
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )
ChelseaSpend









With that much more spent than the rest of the league, lets take a peek at how the experiment has panned out over the last few years:


Code
ChelseaRank <- Ranks |> filter(Squad == "Chelsea")
ggplot() + 
  geom_bump(data=ChelseaRank, aes(x=Season, y=Rank, color="Chelsea")) +
  geom_point(data=ChelseaRank, aes(x=Season, y=Rank, color="Chelsea"), size = 4) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Chelsea" = "#034694"
  )) +
  geom_text(data = ChelseaRank |> filter(Season == min(Season)), aes(x = Season , y=Rank, label = Squad), size = 3, hjust = 1) +
  geom_text(data = ChelseaRank |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x = Season , y=Rank, label = Squad), size = 3, hjust = 0) +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  labs(
  title="Premiere Leauge final placments since\nChampions League win", 
  subtitle="Clearly the project has started to dig them out of a hole,\nhowever money cannot buy trophies and chemistry.", y= "Premiere Leauge Rank",caption="Source: FbRef | By Lucas Krapfl") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    plot.title.position = "plot")









Currently sitting in 6th place, in a horrible run of form, eliminated from the UCL, the face of your team Enzo Fernandez and Cole Palmer wanting to leave this summer, and Financial Fair Play charges looming, it is looking like the blues for the Blues might continue as long as Blueco is at the helm.

title: Kings of Concacaf or Potentially Grouped: Outlook on the USMNT since 2018 Fallout
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)
library(ggbump)
library(ggrepel)
library(gt)
library(ggalt)
library(ggbeeswarm)
UScomplete <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_All_Years_Summary_v2.csv")
US15 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2015_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US16 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2016_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US17 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2017_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US18 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2018_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US19 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2019_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US20 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2020_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US21 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2021_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US22 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2022_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US23 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2023_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US24 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2024_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US25 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2025_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
US26 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_2026_All_Competitions_v2.csv")
USEUPerct <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_European_Pct_By_Year.csv")
USPlayerClubs <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_Players_Clubs_By_Year.csv")
FifaRank <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_Fifa_Rankings_v2.csv")
WCHcomplete <-read_csv("MajorProject2/WCH_All_Years_Summary.csv")
WCHranks <-read_csv("MajorProject2/WCH_Fifa_Rankings.csv")
WCH_Concacaf_ranks <-read_csv("MajorProject2/WCH_CONCACAF_Rankings.csv")
USMNT2018_2024 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_Full_Squad_2018_2024.csv")
Stars2018 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_Stars_2018.csv")
Stars2024 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_Stars_2024.csv")
US2018 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_Full_Squad_2018.csv")
US2024 <- read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_Full_Squad_2024.csv")
EUPlayerCount<-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_European_Player_Count.csv") 
Salaries18v24 <-read_csv("MajorProject2/USMNT_Stars_Salaries.csv")


Everyone remembers the historic failure of the USMNT in 2018, losing to Trinidad and Tobago to miss the World Cup in one of the most embarrassing soccer games ever played. However, the group of players that followed were named the “Golden Generation” of US soccer as we went toward the 2022 World Cup and Beyond.
As we are around 2 months away from a World Cup here in the USA, lets take a look at if the USMNT has truly improved since 2018 and if they have lived up to the nickname of America’s Golden Generation.


Code
ggplot() + 
  geom_bump(data=FifaRank, aes(x=Year, y=Rank, color=Squad)) +
  geom_point(data=FifaRank, aes(x=Year, y=Rank, color=Squad), size = 4) +
scale_y_reverse(breaks = seq(25, 10, -1))+
  labs(title="Offical Fifa world rankings since 2018", subtitle="Ever since the crash and burn in 2018\n you have heard about the USMNT's 'golden generation', and the rankings reflect \nthat but are they accurate of the results?", y= "Fifa World Ranking", x = "Year", caption="Source: FbRef | By Lucas Krapfl") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    plot.title.position = "plot")









Obviously there was a lot of hype coming up to the 2022 world cup, but it has clearly started to fall away. Some of that is coaching turnover, others of it could be losing faith in the young players who didn’t pan out. Lets look at the recent results and see if it backs up the rankings:


Code
team_rows <- 5

season_waffle <- UScomplete |> 
  select(Year, Wins, Losses, Draws) |> 
  pivot_longer(cols=-Year, names_to = "category", values_to ="count") |> 
  uncount(count) |>
  group_by(Year) |> 
  mutate(
    x = (row_number() - 1) %/% team_rows + 1,
    y = (row_number() - 1) %% team_rows + 1
  ) |> 
  ungroup()
SZN21 <- season_waffle |> filter(Year == "2021")
SZN25 <- season_waffle |> filter(Year == "2025")
ggplot() +
  geom_tile(
    data=season_waffle, 
    aes(
      x = x, y = y, fill = category
      ), 
    color = "white",
    linewidth = .5) +
   scale_fill_manual(values = c("Wins" = "darkgreen", "Losses" = "red", "Draws"= "yellow"))+ 
  facet_wrap(~Year, ncol = 6) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(title="USMNT Wins, draws, and losses by year", subtitle="If this truly is the golden generation of US Soccer, surley they will be winning more games than before.\nEspecially since the 2022 World Cup where the excuse was just that they are young....",  caption="Source: FbRef | By Lucas Krapfl") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    plot.title.position = "plot")









Well clearly the results have become sketchy at best. But hey, the one thing USMNT fans have always said is “As soon as we get players into Europe we will be way better and a threat on the global level.” Over the last few years we have seen a lot of US players go to Europe and ride the bench the whole season… not ideal for staying in shape for international duty
New USMNT manager Mauricio Pochetino has made it clear that he is playing players based on there club form. Lets look at the trends of where our players are playing before and after he took over:


Code
USPlayers2025 <- EUPlayerCount |> filter(Year == "2025")
USPlayers2022 <- EUPlayerCount |> filter(Year == "2022")
EUCount<- ggplot() +
  geom_bar(data=EUPlayerCount, aes(x=factor(Year), weight=European_Players), fill="grey")+
  geom_bar(data=USPlayers2025, aes(x=factor(Year), weight=European_Players), fill="green") +
  geom_bar(data=USPlayers2022, aes(x=factor(Year), weight=European_Players), fill="yellow") +
  labs(title="Count of US players in Europe", subtitle="The big complaint with the USMNT was always that our players wer not playing in Europe.\n these top leauges are supposed to be talent farms so lets see how many players we have across the pond.", y= "Count of players In Europe", x = "", caption="Source: FbRef | By Lucas Krapfl") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    plot.title.position = "plot") 
  
  USEUPerctBar <-USEUPerct |> 
  group_by(Year) |> 
  summarise(
    Players_Europe = sum(Europe_Based)/Total_Players *100,
   Players_NonEurope = sum(Non_Europe_Based)/Total_Players *100,
  ) |> 
  select(Year, Players_Europe,Players_NonEurope)
USEUPerctLong <- USEUPerctBar |> 
  arrange(Year) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols=starts_with("Players"),
    names_to="type", 
    values_to="percent")
ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=USEUPerctLong, aes(x=reorder(Year, percent), weight=percent, fill=type)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Players_Europe" = "darkblue", "Players_NonEurope" = "maroon"),
                  labels = c("Players_Europe" = "% USMNT Players in Europe", "Players_NonEurope" = "% USMNT Players not in Europe"))+
  coord_flip()+
  labs(title="Players in European Leagues vs. Non-European", subtitle="The old argument used to be that if the US got players in Europe we would be a threat on the national level\nhowever lets look at the increase in the recent years and how we still have not won anything.", y= "Percent Players Per Leauge", x = "", caption="Source: FbRef | By Lucas Krapfl") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    plot.title.position = "plot")









Code
EUCount + USEUPerctBar









Clearly we have a lot of talent if we are putting that many players in the top leagues. With higher competition comes bigger contracts. Lets look at the compensation the “golden generation” players are getting vs the top 5 players on the disappointing 2018 team


Code
Contracts2024 <- Salaries18v24 |> filter(Year == "2024")
Contracts2018 <- Salaries18v24 |> filter(Year == "2018")
Pulisic2018 <- Contracts2018 |> filter(Player== "Christian Pulisic")
Pulisic2024 <- Contracts2024 |> filter(Player== "Christian Pulisic")
StarSals2018 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=Contracts2018, aes(x=reorder(Player, Annual_Salary_Millions), weight=Annual_Salary_Millions), fill="red") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 8, 1)) +
  geom_bar(data=Pulisic2018, aes(x=reorder(Player, Annual_Salary_Millions), weight=Annual_Salary_Millions), fill="blue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Annual contract value of USMNT stars\n2018 vs 2024",
    subtitle = "Since 2022 Chelsea have signed A LOT of players, but so has everyone else.\nWith all this spending you think there would be some big trophies to show for it...",
    y = "2018 Annual Salary (Millions)",
    x = "",
   caption="Source: TransferMarket | By Lucas Krapfl" )+
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )
StarSals2024 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=Contracts2024, aes(x=reorder(Player, Annual_Salary_Millions), weight=Annual_Salary_Millions), fill="blue") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 8, 1)) +
  geom_bar(data=Pulisic2024, aes(x=reorder(Player, Annual_Salary_Millions), weight=Annual_Salary_Millions), fill="red") +
  coord_flip() +
 labs(
    y = "2024 Annual Salary (Millions)",
    x = "" )+
  theme_minimal()+ 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )
StarSals2018 + StarSals2024









Clearly the median pay for a star has gone up but we do not have that anchor superstar like they had with Michael Bradley. But hey, if the whole team is pretty good and earning big contracts, surely they are putting up big numbers for the USMNT too…


Code
Bees2018 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_beeswarm(
    data=US2018, 
    aes(x=Position, y=Goal_Contributions), color="grey") + 
  geom_beeswarm(
    data=Stars2018, 
    aes(x=Position, y=Goal_Contributions), color="red") + 
 scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 15, 2)) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data=Stars2018, 
    aes(x=Position, y=Goal_Contributions, label=Player))+
    labs(title="USMNT Player Goal Contributions 2018 + 2024", subtitle="This gives us a good look at tactical, personell, and result changes since 2018.\nWith this being the most talented roster ever, lets see how the goal contributions are shared, or lack there of.",  caption="Source: FbRef | By Lucas Krapfl") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    plot.title.position = "plot")
Bees2024 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_beeswarm(
    data=US2024, 
    aes(x=Position, y=Goal_Contributions), color="grey") + 
  geom_beeswarm(
    data=Stars2024, 
    aes(x=Position, y=Goal_Contributions), color="blue") + 
  geom_text_repel(
    data=Stars2024, 
    aes(x=Position, y=Goal_Contributions, label=Player))+
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    plot.title.position = "plot")
Bees2018 + Bees2024









Yikes… A lot of this can be explained by the fact that they do not play in as many games due to their club coaches putting minutes restrictions on the stars. They also play a much more rigid style of football where it is defend first make attacking magic second. They work to not lose rather than flying around and seeing what happens in the past. This has been a trend with the international game as a whole
Lastly, with this change in style lets take a look at the goal differential over the last few years to visualize the change in play.


Code
ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=WCHcomplete, aes(x=Year, y=Goal_Differential, group=Squad), color="grey") +
  geom_line(data=UScomplete, aes(x=Year, y=Goal_Differential), color="blue") + 
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-20, 50))+
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(2018, 2026, 1), limits = c(2018, 2026)) +
  labs(title="USMNT goal differential vs Mexico + Canada", subtitle="Lets see how the World Cup hosts have stacked up in goal differential since 2018.\nIf the US is truly the so called Kings of Concacaf, surley they will have much better stats",  caption="Source: FbRef | By Lucas Krapfl",
       x="",
       y="Goal Differential") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    plot.title.position = "plot")









Clearly, Concacaf struggles with stability. With all three of these teams hosting World Cup games in a month, the storylines will be interesting to follow. Does the USMNT finally rise into their Golden Generation status with a deep run, or do they burn out like the 2024 Copa America and 2018 WCQ? Does Mexico bring back their storied past and rise from the rubble of the rough seasons they have been through recently? Does Canada continue their miracle ascension and spark what would be one of the fastest rises of a team ever? In one month and change, we will have these answers and even more questions for some.
